content:
# Acts Chapter 8 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 234-235 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_008_page_001.jpg, ACT_008_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And Saul was consenting to his killing. And there arose on that day a great persecution against the church in Jerusalem, and they were all scattered throughout the regions of Judea and Samaria, except the apostles.

**2** And devout men carried Stephen to burial and made great lamentation over him.

**3** But Saul was ravaging the church, entering house after house, and dragging off both men and women, he delivered them to prison.

**4** Now those who had been scattered went about proclaiming the good news of the word.

**5** And Philip, having gone down to the city of Samaria, was proclaiming the Christ to them.

**6** And the crowds with one accord were giving heed to the things spoken by Philip, as they heard and saw the signs which he was doing.

**7** For unclean spirits, crying out with a loud voice, were coming out of many who had them, and many who were paralyzed and lame were healed.

**8** And there was great joy in that city.

**9** Now a certain man named Simon had previously been practicing sorcery in the city and astonishing the people of Samaria, claiming to be someone great.

**10** To whom they all were giving heed, from the least to the greatest, saying, "This one is the Power of God, the one called Great."

**11** And they were giving heed to him because for a considerable time he had been astonishing them with his sorceries.

**12** But when they believed Philip as he was proclaiming the good news concerning the kingdom of God and the name of Jesus Christ, they were being baptized, both men and women.

**13** And Simon himself also believed, and having been baptized, he was continuing steadfastly with Philip, and beholding signs and great works of power taking place, he was amazed.

**14** Now when the apostles in Jerusalem heard that Samaria had received the word of God, they sent to them Peter and John,

**15** who, having come down, prayed for them that they might receive the Holy Spirit.

**16** For it had not yet fallen upon any of them; they had only been baptized into the name of the Lord Jesus.

**17** Then they were laying their hands upon them, and they were receiving the Holy Spirit.

**18** Now when Simon saw that through the laying on of the hands of the apostles the Spirit was given, he offered them money,

**19** saying, "Give to me also this authority, so that upon whomever I lay my hands, he may receive the Holy Spirit."

**20** But Peter said to him, "May your silver perish with you, because you supposed you could obtain the gift of God with money.

**21** There is for you neither part nor share in this matter, for your heart is not right before God.

**22** Repent therefore of this wickedness of yours, and beseech the Lord, if perhaps the intention of your heart may be forgiven you.

**23** For I see that you are in the gall of bitterness and the bond of unrighteousness."

**24** And Simon answered and said, "Beseech the Lord on my behalf, so that nothing of what you have spoken may come upon me."

**25** They then, having solemnly testified and spoken the word of the Lord, were returning to Jerusalem, and were proclaiming the good news to many villages of the Samaritans.

**26** And an angel of the Lord spoke to Philip, saying, "Rise and go toward the south, upon the road that goes down from Jerusalem to Gaza." This is a desert road.

**27** And he rose and went. And behold, a man, an Ethiopian eunuch, a court official of Candace, queen of the Ethiopians, who was over all her treasury, who had come to Jerusalem to worship,

**28** and was returning and sitting in his chariot, and was reading the prophet Isaiah.

**29** And the Spirit said to Philip, "Approach and join yourself to this chariot."

**30** And Philip, having run up, heard him reading Isaiah the prophet, and said, "Do you understand what you are reading?"

**31** And he said, "How could I, unless someone should guide me?" And he invited Philip to come up and sit with him.

**32** Now the passage of the Scripture which he was reading was this: "As a sheep he was led to the slaughter, and as a lamb before its shearer is silent, so he does not open his mouth.

**33** In his humiliation his judgment was taken away. His generation, who shall declare? For his life is taken from the earth."

**34** And the eunuch answered Philip and said, "I ask you, concerning whom does the prophet say this -- concerning himself or concerning someone else?"

**35** And Philip, having opened his mouth and beginning from this Scripture, proclaimed to him the good news of Jesus.

**36** And as they were going along the road, they came upon some water, and the eunuch said, "Behold, water! What hinders me from being baptized?"

**37** [This verse is absent from Codex Sinaiticus.]

**38** And he commanded the chariot to stand still, and they both went down into the water, both Philip and the eunuch, and he baptized him.

**39** And when they came up out of the water, the Spirit of the Lord snatched Philip away, and the eunuch saw him no more, for he was going on his way rejoicing.

**40** But Philip was found at Azotus, and passing through, he was proclaiming the good news to all the cities until he came to Caesarea.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Acts 8 marks the explosive expansion of the gospel beyond Jerusalem -- first to Samaria (vv. 1-25), then to an Ethiopian eunuch (vv. 26-40). The chapter pivots on the theme of who may receive the Spirit and how, with Simon Magus serving as a cautionary counterpoint to genuine faith.

**Manuscript condition:** Both pages of Codex Sinaiticus (234-235) are well-preserved and legible in the four-column scriptio continua format. The text runs from the end of Acts 7 through to Acts 9:7. Standard nomina sacra are used throughout. No significant scribal corrections were observed affecting the translation.

**Agreement among translators:** The three translators show remarkably high agreement on the narrative structure and basic meaning of this chapter. All three correctly identify the absence of verse 37, the Sinaiticus reading of "the Spirit" (without "Holy") in v.18, the preposition EIS ("into") in v.16, and "Spirit of the Lord" in v.39. The major differences are stylistic rather than substantive.

**General translation approach:** SOPHIA has followed LOGOS most closely in this chapter, as its formal approach most faithfully preserves the Greek syntax and vocabulary while remaining accessible English. EMET's hyper-literal renderings (Hierosolyma, Philippos, Iesous, Esaias) are appropriate for a literal translation but overly defamiliarizing for a consensus text. PNEUMA's dynamic renderings are vivid but occasionally depart too freely from the Greek (e.g., "explosion of joy" for chara megale, "poisoned with bitterness and shackled by sin" for the Hebraic genitives in v.23).

**Key consensus decisions:**

1. **Proper names:** Standard English forms (Philip, Peter, John, Jesus, Isaiah, Jerusalem, Caesarea) rather than EMET's transliterations. This follows the SOPHIA convention established in prior chapters.

2. **"Church" vs. "assembly" (ekklesia):** SOPHIA uses "church" (with LOGOS and PNEUMA) rather than EMET's "assembly." While "assembly" is etymologically accurate, "church" is the established English term for the Christian ekklesia in Acts.

3. **"Baptized" vs. "immersed":** SOPHIA uses "baptized" (with LOGOS and PNEUMA) rather than EMET's "immersed." The transliteration "baptize" has become an English word with precise theological meaning; replacing it with "immerse" is interpretive rather than translational.

4. **v.5 -- "proclaiming the Christ":** SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS in retaining "the Christ" (with the article) rather than PNEUMA's "the Messiah." Both are valid, but "the Christ" preserves the Greek form ton Christon and maintains continuity with the rest of the New Testament translation.

5. **v.13 -- "works of power" (dunameis):** SOPHIA follows LOGOS's "great works of power" to preserve the verbal echo with v.10 where Simon is called "the Power (dunamis) of God." This connection is lost if rendered "miracles."

6. **v.16 -- "into the name" (eis to onoma):** SOPHIA preserves the Sinaiticus preposition EIS ("into") rather than harmonizing to "in the name." This is confirmed by the manuscript image on page 234. The distinction between EIS (into, direction/incorporation) and EN (in, sphere/authority) is theologically significant for baptismal theology. LOGOS rendered this as "in the name," departing from the Sinaiticus reading; SOPHIA corrects this to "into the name" following EMET and PNEUMA.

7. **v.18 -- "the Spirit" (to pneuma):** SOPHIA follows the Sinaiticus reading "the Spirit" without "Holy," as confirmed in the manuscript. All three translators correctly noted this variant. The surrounding context (vv. 15, 17, 19) uses "Holy Spirit," making the bare "Spirit" in v.18 a distinctive reading that should not be harmonized.

8. **v.23 -- Hebraic genitives:** SOPHIA preserves the Hebraic genitive constructions "gall of bitterness" and "bond of unrighteousness" (with EMET and LOGOS) rather than PNEUMA's dynamic "poisoned with bitterness and shackled by sin." These phrases echo Deuteronomy 29:18 and Isaiah 58:6 (LXX), and the biblical resonance should be preserved.

9. **v.33 -- "generation" (genea):** SOPHIA retains "generation" (with EMET and LOGOS) rather than PNEUMA's "descendants." The Greek genea in Isaiah 53:8 (LXX) is deliberately ambiguous -- it may refer to contemporaries, descendants, or manner of life. "Generation" preserves this productive ambiguity.

10. **v.37 -- Absent verse:** All three translators correctly note the absence of this verse from Codex Sinaiticus. SOPHIA follows suit. The verse (the eunuch's confession of faith) is a later Western interpolation absent from all major early witnesses (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus, Alexandrinus, P45).

11. **v.39 -- "snatched ... away" (herpasen):** SOPHIA uses "snatched" (with EMET and LOGOS) rather than PNEUMA's "swept." The Greek herpasen conveys violent, sudden removal -- the same verb used of the Spirit "catching up" people in eschatological contexts (cf. 1 Thess 4:17). "Snatched" preserves this force.

---

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | Tone of persecution | "came-about ... a great persecution" | "there arose ... a great persecution" | "a brutal wave of persecution crashed down" | Follows LOGOS; PNEUMA adds intensity not in the Greek |
| 4 | Proclamation verb | "proclaiming-good-news [with] the word" | "proclaiming the word" | "announcing the good news of the word" | "proclaiming the good news of the word" -- the Greek euangelizomenoi requires the "good news" element |
| 5 | "Christ" vs. "Messiah" | "the Christ" | "the Christ" | "the Messiah" | "the Christ" -- 2-to-1 agreement, preserves Greek form |
| 5 | "proclaiming" vs. "preaching" | "was proclaiming" (kerusson) | "was preaching" (kerusson) | "began proclaiming" | "was proclaiming" -- LOGOS's own note #12 distinguishes kerusson from euangelizomenos |
| 7 | Syntax of exorcisms | Preserves anacoluthon | Smooths syntax | Smooths syntax | Follows LOGOS's smoothed reading for clarity while keeping close to Greek |
| 8 | "great joy" rendering | "great joy" | "great joy" | "explosion of joy" | "great joy" -- literal Greek; PNEUMA's metaphor is interpretive |
| 10 | Title phrasing | "the Power of God, the [one] being called Great" | "the power of God which is called Great" | "the Power of God, the one called Great" | "the Power of God, the one called Great" -- preserves the title with EMET/PNEUMA's phrasing |
| 13 | dunameis rendering | "great powers" | "great works of power" | "extraordinary displays of power" | "great works of power" -- preserves dunamis echo from v.10 |
| 16 | Preposition: EIS vs. EN | "into the name" (EIS) | "in the name" (EN) | "into the name" (EIS) | "into the name" -- Sinaiticus reads EIS, confirmed in manuscript |
| 18 | "the Spirit" vs. "Holy Spirit" | "the Spirit" | "the Spirit" | "the Spirit" | "the Spirit" -- all three agree, following Sinaiticus |
| 20 | Curse formulation | "may-it-be unto destruction" | "May your silver perish with you" | "May your silver go to destruction with you" | "May your silver perish with you" -- LOGOS captures the optative force idiomatically |
| 21 | "word" vs. "matter" | "this word" (logos) | "this matter" | "this work" | "this matter" -- logos here means "matter/affair," not a spoken word |
| 23 | Hebraic genitives | "gall of bitterness ... bond of unrighteousness" | "gall of bitterness ... bond of unrighteousness" | "poisoned with bitterness ... shackled by sin" | Preserves Hebraic genitives with EMET/LOGOS; biblical allusions must be retained |
| 26 | "desert" referent | "This is a desert [place]" | "This is a desert road" | Integrated into sentence | "This is a desert road" -- follows LOGOS; the gloss likely describes the road |
| 27 | "powerful-one" vs. "court official" | "a powerful-one of Kandake" (dunastes) | "a court official of Candace" | "a court official of the Kandake" | "a court official of Candace" -- dunastes here means an official, not merely "powerful one" |
| 33 | "generation" vs. "descendants" | "the generation of him" | "His generation" | "his descendants" | "His generation" -- preserves the deliberate ambiguity of genea |
| 34 | Question phrasing | "Concerning himself, or concerning some other?" | "concerning himself or concerning someone else?" | "Himself, or someone else?" | "concerning himself or concerning someone else?" -- follows LOGOS/EMET's fuller form |
| 39 | Verb for Spirit's action | "snatched-away" | "snatched ... away" | "swept ... away" | "snatched ... away" -- herpasen means forceful seizure, not gentle sweeping |
| 39 | Eunuch's joy | "going on his way rejoicing" | "going on his way rejoicing" | "went on his way overflowing with joy" | "going on his way rejoicing" -- 2-to-1; the Greek is simply eporeueto chairon |
